Analyst estimates have increased for ADT. In the beginning of the year, the average EPS estimate for 2026 was $0.93. Now the estimate is $0.97. The peer comparison further supports the case for multiple expansion presented in the previous article. Applying a P/E multiple of 12-14x would translate to a share price of $11.6 to $13.6, representing an upside of over 33%. Considering ADT’s business profile and now Verisure’s valuation, it is not unjustified to expect multiple expansion for ADT.

There are five analysts following ADT with an average target price of $9.6.

Capital allocation has been leaning towards buybacks

ADT has not increased the dividend since the large in March 2024. Currently the shares yield 2.5%. Payout ratio remains low at 26%, according to Seeking Alpha. Combined with buybacks, the shareholder yield reaches up to 11%. ADT has purchased back 65 million shares in 2025, and it has a little over a hundred million left in its share repurchase plan. Considering the fact that ADT has significant debt maturities in the following years, ADT could reduce its debt and deliver more earnings to investors pockets instead of debtors.

Conclusion

ADT’s share price is supported by three levers. Its business is performing well, and it is reducing the debt and doing buybacks. Apollo’s ownership share has approximately halved down to 22%, according to Koyfin. The disposal of shares by Apollo could still represent an opportunity to increase position in ADT if the shares decline as a result of temporary oversupply. The listing of ADT’s European cousin further highlights ADT’s low valuation.

data.title: Chewy: Accelerating Growth, Rising Margins (Upgrade)
data.description:
It's a tricky stock market right now, with valuations stretched and investors poised to sell on the smallest whiff of a negative headline. In this environment, I continue to emphasize allocating out of large-cap tech stocks and into more small- and mid-cap names that have their own independent catalysts to beat the broader market.

Chewy (NYSE:CHWY), in my view, is looking increasingly attractive. The pet supplies e-commerce outlet has showcased accelerating revenue growth in a relatively weaker consumer environment, showcasing the countercyclical nature of its business. At the same time, the company is showcasing meaningful strides in its adjusted EBITDA, helping to justify its valuation. Moreover, the stock's ~15% rise this year hasn't been outlandish, giving investors a perfect entry point while the stock is still trading at reasonable levels.

I last wrote a "Neutral" opinion on Chewy in July, when the stock was trading at similar levels just under $40. Since then, Chewy has attempted (and failed) to rebound, but at the same time, the company is looking fundamentally stronger with healthy growth rates, rising gross margins that showcase deft management of tariffs, and an expanding bottom line. Due to a combination of Chewy's rising fundamental appeal plus the fact that fewer reasonably-priced growth alternatives are available in today's stock market, I'm upgrading Chewy to a "Buy" rating.

To me, here are the core reasons to be bullish on Chewy:

  • Clear category leader with recurring autoship revenue. Chewy's reputation for stellar customer service has earned it the loyalty of many pet owners, while "autoship" orders represent over 80% of Chewy's business, building a recurring revenue base.
  • Rising gross margin profile. The company is benefiting from sponsored ads as well as a rising mix shift into premium products, raising the company's gross margin profile at a time when many retail companies are struggling under the burden of tariffs.
  • Countercyclical demand profile. Chewy's accelerating revenue growth is ample proof of the fact that pet owners are quite resilient and willing to spend on their pets even in the wake of a softer economy.
  • New revenue streams. The company is diversifying its business into tertiary new revenue streams, including a greater focus on its $49/year Chewy+ membership plan as well as new pet health insurance offerings.
  • Clean balance sheet. The company has ~$0.6 billion of cash unencumbered by debt, giving Chewy plenty of financial flexibility to pursue growth.

Despite these strengths, amid Chewy's consistent and reliable guidance/margin increases, we think Chewy trades at a reasonable, though not exactly bargain, price. At current share prices near $39, Chewy stock sits at a market cap of $16.08 billion. Netting off the $591.8 million of cash on Chewy's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $15.49 billion.

For FY25, as shown below, Chewy has boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $12.5-$12.6 billion in sales (5%-6% growth), versus a 4%-5% growth range previously. The 5.7% high end of the company's adjusted EBITDA range (still conservative given 1H margins came in higher) gives us an adjusted EBITDA of $715.4 million against the midpoint of the company's revenue guide.

For FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Chewy to hit $13.60 billion in revenue, or an acceleration to 8% y/y growth (which is actually in line with current growth trends). If we conservatively assume a ~50bps adjusted EBITDA margin expansion next year (about half of the company's current trend of expanding margins at ~1 point y/y), adjusted EBITDA would be $843.2 million (+18% y/y).

This positions Chewy's valuation multiples at:

  • 21.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 18.4x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

In my view, paying a mid/high teens adjusted EBITDA multiple for a company that has recurring revenue, new higher-margin revenue streams, a track record for expanding gross margins, and is currently growing adjusted EBITDA at a >20% clip remains quite a good bargain.

To me, the combination of a choppy/receding share price since summer, plus the improving sales trends, has opened up an attractive buying opportunity here. Stay long here and hold out for a bigger rebound in Chewy stock.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Chewy's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Chewy's revenue grew 8.6% y/y to $3.10 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.08 billion (+7.7% y/y) by a one-point margin. Even more impressively yet, as we note in the chart below, Chewy's revenue accelerated at a slight 30bps y/y pace relative to 8.3% growth in Q1. Though the acceleration in itself isn't large, we note that most retail companies have experienced a sharp contraction in revenue and same-store sales growth, a reflection of a weakening consumer economy. Chewy's relative strength in keeping up its growth rates offers compelling evidence that pet owners don't trade down and stop paying for premium food and supplies, even when their budgets are tighter elsewhere.

Autoship sales grew at a much faster 14.9% y/y pace to $2.58 billion, also accelerating 10bps versus Q1's autoship growth rate. Autoship is now 83% of overall sales, a five-point increase versus an already-high 78% in the year-ago Q2. The increasing mix of Autoship orders is one of the key drivers behind Chewy's resilience: sheer inertia and the comfort in buying repeat orders are partially protecting Chewy's revenue and giving the company a higher degree of visibility versus other retail companies with much more variable sales trends.

Increasing the spotlight on Chewy+ memberships ($49/year) is becoming more and more critical to the company's retention strategy. Though Chewy notes that Chewy+ sales represent a minimal slice of overall revenue, the company has noted that members are substantially increasing their spending and frequency of using Chewy. The program also makes it less likely for customers to defect to rivals like Amazon (AMZN) or in-store pet retail outlets.

Per CEO Sumit Singh's remarks on the company's development of the Chewy+ program on the Q2 earnings call:

In the month of July, roughly 3% of Chewy's total monthly sales were to Chewy+ members. Importantly, we are observing strong incrementality in spend, NSPAC and positive contribution profit per customer across Chewy+ customers compared to nonmembers. Furthermore, other key leading indicators of success are promising. These customers are buying at a higher frequency and attaching a higher number of products to their orders.

Additionally, we are observing incremental Autoship adoption and greater mobile app usage from Chewy+ members relative to nonmembers. All of this is leading to both higher as well as accelerated NSPAC curves for Chewy+ customers compared to nonmembers, which in turn is contributing positively to Chewy's net sales flywheel. As we exit this year, we expect approximately mid-single-digit percentage of our net sales to go through the Chewy+ program.

On the profitability front, Chewy's pro forma gross margins rose 90bps y/y to 30.4%, which the company attributed to customers gravitating toward more premium products as well as a greater contribution from Chewy-sponsored ads. Again, Chewy's rising margin profile makes the company a notable outlier in a retail environment battered by tariffs.

Adjusted EBITDA, meanwhile, soared 26% y/y to $183 million. The company's adjusted EBITDA margins improved 80bps y/y, capitalizing on the improvement in gross margins.

Again, while Chewy isn't exactly cheap at a mid/high teens adjusted EBITDA multiple, we should consider the pace of Chewy's profit expansion as a core reason to invest in the stock at a premium - especially considering the company has multiple drivers to continue pushing growth and margins higher (Chewy+, Chewy Vet Care, premium store brands).

Key takeaways

In my view, Chewy has become an excellent stock to diversify our growth portfolio into. Chewy benefits from multiple countercyclical demand tailwinds that are driving improved revenue growth, allowing the company to achieve 20%+ adjusted EBITDA expansion. Stay long here and "Buy" on dips.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Mixed Setup With Limited Near-Term Catalysts
data.description:
I last covered Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) in July with a neutral rating, as I was concerned about its muted near-term growth prospects. The stock has underperformed the S&P 500 (SPY) since then, declining mid-single digits. Moving forward, PG’s growth outlook remains mixed. The consumer backdrop remains challenging, which should continue to weigh on sales and volume trends. There are also near-term headwinds from retail inventory reductions and the broader shift toward online and club channels, which tend to run leaner inventories. On top of that, the company is discontinuing certain product lines as part of its restructuring, which could create short-term revenue drag. Pricing and mix should support revenue, but given the challenging consumer environment, I will wait and watch to see how much traction the pricing moves actually gets.

Margins is a similar story. Tariffs are a clear near-term headwind, and ongoing pricing actions should help to offset their impact to some extent. However, we need to watch the pricing traction in the near term. Over the medium term, productivity programs and cost-efficiency initiatives should start to kick in more visibly, helping margins..

P&G’s valuation looks reasonable at a discount to historical levels. I don’t see much downside, but it’s hard to make a strong upside case until the macro turns more visible and we have more clarity on pricing traction. I’d stay patient here and maintain a Neutral view on the stock for now.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Procter & Gamble delivered 2% organic sales growth in Q4 FY2025. Volumes were flat, while pricing and mix each contributed roughly one point of growth. The performance was fairly broad-based, with nine out of ten product categories holding or growing organic sales during the quarter. Geographically, results were mixed. Focus markets grew 1%, with North America flat and Europe up 2%. Enterprise markets showed stronger momentum at 3% growth, led by Latin America, while Greater China improved sequentially, posting 2% growth after several weak quarters.

P&G’s near-term revenue outlook is mixed. The macro environment is still tough, with tariffs, inflation, and high interest rates keeping consumer confidence fragile, especially in the U.S.

The company has implemented mid-single-digit price increases across about a quarter of its portfolio in the U.S. to offset tariff costs. So, price/cost should be positive. However, since the macro environment is tough, it remains to be seen the extent of price increases on volumes. Since most of the price increase will hit in the first quarter, it will be a good test of price elasticity. The company will report Q1 earnings later this month, and I expect volumes to likely stay soft as consumers adjust to higher prices.

Consumers and retailers are still cautious and stocking less products. In addition, the continued shift toward online channels/club stores (which are inventory efficient) means less on-hand inventory across the system. That’s creating a bit of a gap between sell-in and sell-out trends, and I expect it to continue negatively impacting sales in the coming quarters.

Another near-term drag comes from the company’s two-year restructuring program. Simplifying the portfolio and existing lower-growth/margin categories should shave about 30 to 50 basis points off FY26 organic sales growth.

The second half should look better. As pricing is fully absorbed, and restructuring savings start to flow into reinvestment in innovation and marketing, growth should stabilize. However, for now, it’s a bit of a wait-and-see story. Near-term headwinds are still in play, but if the macro backdrop improves and new launches deliver, the setup could turn more constructive later in the year.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

P&G’s margin performance in the Q4FY25 was mixed. Core gross margin declined 70 basis points, mainly reflecting higher commodity costs and unfavorable geographic mix. Despite that, core operating margin improved 150 basis points, supported by strong productivity savings and disciplined cost control.

The company delivered roughly 560 basis points of productivity improvements, which helped more than offset input cost inflation and higher supply chain expenses. These gains came from ongoing cost savings programs, manufacturing efficiencies, and overhead reduction initiatives. Even with a step-up in brand investment and innovation spending, P&G still managed to post operating margin growth. On a currency-neutral basis, the core operating margin increase was even stronger, up around 170 basis points year over year.

P&G’s margin outlook looks mixed in the near term, with tariffs standing out as the main headwind. Management expects about a billion dollars in pre-tax tariff costs in FY26, with roughly $200 million from materials and products imported from China to the U.S., another $200 million from Canada’s tariffs on U.S. goods, and around $600 million from tariffs on other global shipments into the U.S. This will likely weigh on both gross and operating margins, especially early in the year.

The company has been taking price increases across parts of its portfolio to help offset those costs. As those pricing actions start to gain traction, and elasticity stabilizes, margins should improve as the year progresses. There’s also a chance of some relief if trade tensions ease or tariffs are rolled back, though that still feels uncertain. For now, it’s fair to expect the first half to be bumpy, with some stabilization later as pricing gains traction.

Looking further out, the company’s ongoing productivity work should be a bigger driver of margin improvement. P&G’s “Supply Chain 3.0” program is a large-scale, tech-led effort aimed at driving up to $1.5 billion in pre-tax savings. The plan focuses on digitization and automation across plants, warehouses, and logistics - things like advanced warehouse systems that boost storage density by roughly 50% and increase throughput two to three times, and real-time, touchless quality systems that improve efficiency and cut costs across manufacturing sites.

The company is also testing automation-heavy “dark shift” operations that let plants run unattended overnight using robotics. Early results from its Berlin site have been encouraging, showing productivity gains between 15% and 60%, while also improving worker satisfaction since fewer people have to work night shifts. If scaled, that could deliver meaningful structural cost savings over the next few years.

Outside manufacturing, the two-year restructuring plan should also help margins over time. P&G plans to eliminate roughly 7,000 non-manufacturing roles, streamline its organization, and exit lower-margin or slower-growth categories and markets. These moves will create some near-term noise but should strengthen operating leverage longer term.

Overall, near-term margin pressure from tariffs and restructuring costs will likely keep results uneven for a while. But the medium- to long-term setup looks better. Pricing momentum, productivity programs, and a leaner operating structure should gradually rebuild margin strength through the back half of FY26 and beyond.

Valuation and Rating

Procter & Gamble currently trades at 21.60x FY26 consensus EPS of $6.98 and 20.41x FY27 EPS of $7.39. That’s a discount to its five-year average forward P/E of roughly 24.43x. I think this discount is warranted given the near-term growth outlook and broader macro uncertainty.

Earnings growth is expected to stay muted in the near term, with around 2.14% year over year for FY26. Both revenue and margin growth are being weighed down by tariffs, cost inflation, and a sluggish consumer backdrop. Longer term, the company is taking the right steps with restructuring, portfolio simplification, and productivity investments, but it’ll likely take some macro tailwinds before those efforts translate into a more meaningful earnings acceleration. Until then, high-single-digit EPS growth still looks out of reach.

The current valuation does provide some downside protection, but near-term upside catalysts remain limited. Execution risk around productivity savings, consumer response to pricing, and continued pressure from private-label competition could all keep sentiment cautious. On the other hand, expectations heading into this quarter’s results are already pretty muted, and with the sell-side estimating EPS decline in Q1, much of the bad news is arguably priced in. That probably caps the downside, but it also makes it hard to build a strong bullish case right now.

If the macro backdrop improves in the back half and pricing or cost initiatives begin to gain more traction, there’s room for the setup to turn more constructive. But at least heading into this earnings print, I don’t see a near-term catalyst that can meaningfully re-rate the stock higher. Given the muted earnings growth and lack of visibility on improvement, I’d stay on the sidelines for now and maintain a Neutral rating.

data.title: Nomad Foods: Historically Cheap As Weather And Inflation Weigh On Results
data.description:
Weather- and inflation-related headwinds appear to have taken their toll on Nomad Foods (NYSE:NOMD) this year, with 2025 set to mark the end of its previously consistent track record of revenue and earnings growth. As a result, shares of Europe's leading savory frozen foods firm have fallen by around 25% since my last update in January – a significantly worse performance than the wider global staples space.

While Nomad is serving up some relatively weak results right now, the market has well and truly responded, sending its valuation to just 6.5x current-year consensus EPS. With this representing a huge discount to its past average and not requiring any growth to justify, I am upgrading the shares a notch to "Strong Buy."

Nomad Foods Recap

Nomad may not be familiar to U.S. readers given its geographic mix, but brands like Birds Eye, Findus, iglo, and the Goodfella's pizza range will resonate more with readers in Europe. Indeed, based on retail sales, Nomad is the No. 2 frozen foods player on the continent after Unilever's (UL) ice cream business.

Per above, Nomad's products skew largely to the savory category, specifically vegetables and protein (fish in particular), although it does have exposure to ice creams and desserts via the Frikom and Ledo brands. The company's overall share of the savory frozen food market is somewhere in the high-teens area in the markets it operates in, but that rises significantly once you drill deeper into specific sub-categories like frozen fish. The company's most important markets are the U.K. (~28% of sales), Italy (~13%), Germany (~12%), and France (~7%).

Guidance Lowered On Volume Weakness & Cost Inflation

For many years, Nomad's financial performance looked as stable as its business description would suggest. Indeed, revenue, EBITDA, and EPS mostly grew like clockwork between 2016 and 2024.

This year is shaping up to be weaker for Nomad. Over the first six months, it booked just over €1.5 billion in revenue, down around 2.5% on an organic basis. Soft volume has been the principal driver of that, also down around 2.5% year-on-year.

A couple of things appear to have happened. Firstly, Nomad saw retailers work down their inventories in the first quarter, with organic revenue down 3.6% in that period on a similar drop in volume. The actual sell-through of its products to consumers was stronger, up 0.2% in that quarter, albeit that was still below the category average. In fairness, management did hint earlier in the year that Nomad would lose market share in the first quarter. This was simply a result of the timing of its advertising and promotional initiatives, which were skewed more toward the end of Q1 and into Q2.

On top of that, the macro backdrop in key markets like the U.K. isn't great right now. Consumer sentiment is poor, and Nomad's brands, while ubiquitous, don't have particularly strong pricing power. That leaves the company susceptible to down-trading into private label brands.

Q2 wasn't great for the firm either. Sales fell around 1% to €747 million on a similar drop in volume. I am less worried about this performance given that weather-related weakness goes a long way to explaining this, with Europe seeing record-high temperatures toward the end of the quarter.

While blaming the weather may seem like an excuse from management, you can see in the chart below that the entire category dipped. The simple fact is that appetites tend to decline when temperatures are very hot, and with Nomad's product mix skewing heavily toward things like coated fish, it was simply not well-placed to outperform. Consistent with this, the frozen category has seen better trends in more recent periods, shown below, and this should be reflected in the company's H2 revenue performance.

Still, lower first-half revenue has naturally weighed on Nomad's earnings. Additionally, the company has seen extra pressure come from the cost line, with management mentioning on the last earnings call that inflation is running around 200 basis points above its initial forecast. Since Nomad typically negotiates prices with retailers at the start of the year, it hasn't been able to claw this back yet. While revenue declined in H1, COGS were roughly flat at €1.09 billion. As a result, adjusted gross profit fell 6% to €419 million, with the firm's gross margin contracting by 110 basis points to 27.8%. Adjusted EPS declined 9% to €0.74.

This poor performance has led management to cut full-year guidance heavily. Whereas it previously expected revenue, EBITDA, and EPS to grow, it now sees declines across the board.

Shares Now Trade For Less Than 7x Consensus Earnings

Nomad's shares trade for $12.50 as I type. Excluding dividends, they have fallen by around 25% so far this year. Now, while management has walked down its 2025 EPS guidance by around 10% since my last piece, the U.S. dollar has also fallen by a roughly similar amount against the euro. As a result, Nomad's price-to-earnings ratio has contracted from ~9x to just 6.5x consensus 2025 EPS. In theory, the stock would yield around 15% if Nomad simply returned all of its earnings to investors.

Unsurprisingly, Nomad's P/E ratio also looks very cheap compared to past average levels. Based on year-end levels, its shares have typically traded for around 12.5x adjusted EPS, equal to a more reasonable 8% earnings yield.

It's true that the P/E ratio does mask a degree of leverage. Nomad had around €1.8 billion in net debt at the end of H1 2025, equal to roughly 3.4x its adjusted annual EBITDA. This doesn't really alter the point. At roughly 7.5x, Nomad's EV/EBITDA multiple is also at a substantial discount to its long-run average (~10x).

Nomad pays a $0.17 per share quarterly dividend, equal to around €0.15. The yield is now over 5%. Even with reduced 2025 EPS guidance, this still only implies a circa 35% payout ratio. The company still expects to convert 90% of adjusted net income into free cash flow, leaving plenty of surplus cash after paying its dividend.

Given this, Nomad has been aggressively buying back stock. Shares outstanding ended Q2 at around 150 million, down around 4% from the start of the year. The impact of this on EPS has been masked by how weak H1 was, but it will help in the back half of the year and beyond:

Nomad looks like a solid value play. Financial results are currently soft due to macro issues, but its valuation is now so low that it doesn't require any long-term growth to justify. Accordingly, I am upgrading my rating to "Strong Buy."

data.title: TECL: Risks And Rewards Of The Leveraged Bull
data.description:
The Direxion Daily Technology Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:TECL) is an aggressive ETF that uses economic leverage in the form of swaps and futures to derive a 300% return on the assets held and on a daily reckoning. These assets, as the name suggests, are chosen from a tech-focused index, and it's called The Technology Select Sector Index (IXTTR) from S&P Global.

What Do We Know About TECL?

TECL and its inverse peer, the Direxion Daily Technology Bear 3X Shares ETF (TECS), are just two among dozens and dozens of broad and specific leveraged ETFs floated by Direxion over the past 17 or so years. The first wave of these made its appearance in 2008, and it included TECL and TECS. They're basically the long and the short side of the technology trade, and they work the same way to create leverage, but with diametrically opposite goals.

The fund's ER stands at 0.91%, so it's kind of middle-of-the-road if you look at the spectrum of ETF costs in this active niche. You may see a figure of 0.85% on sites like Seeking Alpha and other financial portals, which is net of a few other expenses such as AFFE. That's basically "Acquired Fund Fees and expenses," which you'd normally see in a fund of funds ETF. This is a non-cash component that shareholders of TECL don't actually pay for, but the SEC requires (see under "Other Terms to Know" in the linked page) the opex of any fund held by another to reflect on the latter's gross expense ratio. I still don't know how we get to that 0.85% because TECL's fact sheet says this:

If Acquired Fund Fees and Expenses were excluded, the Net Expense Ratio would be 0.89% for TECL.

For now, investors won't need to worry about the ER increasing too much, and this comes from the fact that it's capped at 0.95% until September 1, 2026, due to waivers from Rafferty Asset Management, the adviser.

Where Do the Risks and Rewards Lie?

Heading over to the risk considerations, the big-ticket item here is the extrapolated volatility of nearly 80%. That should be kind of obvious because we're talking about a sector that's been extremely volatile over the past few years, ever since the launch of the first globally available public version of a generative artificial intelligence program, a.k.a. ChatGPT.

AI is now firmly in the driver's seat for the tech segment, but valuations are so far removed from fundamentals that investors today don't bat an eyelid when paying 60x or 90x cash flows for the best performers.

There's a ton of risks at these levels, and you don't need to go very far to see the flip side of this – that is, how much riskier it is to use leverage to bet against the market. The Direxion Daily Technology Bear 3X Shares ETF (TECS), the bearish sibling of TECL, is performing so badly that it has lost 80% of its asset value in just the last six months.

Fact of the matter is, TECS has performed so poorly and for the longest time that it is still a $110 million AUM fund after being around for 17 years. Even worse, look at the number of reverse stock splits it's been forced to undergo since its inception in 2008.

Now, that's not any proof that TECL is a great ETF, but it's more than crystal clear that tech is not a good sector to bet against for long. TECS is a great tool for prolonged tech drawdowns, but that's not something we've seen that often. As a proxy, let's look at two very broad unleveraged tech ETFs, The Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) and the iShares US Technology ETF (IYW).

For sure, there have been major pullbacks and minor corrections along the way, but the general momentum has been upward for the better part of the existence of these tech funds.

What's intrinsically relevant to TECL here is the fact that these high-priced stocks often drive the market, and they create the high implied volatility required for a derivatives strategy to work well.

Composition, Strategy, and Performance Analysis

In spite of the fund being leveraged to a daily 3x return on the underlying index, the derivatives angle doesn't directly involve TECL's equity holdings. You can download daily holdings at this link, and you'll see that the ETF holds swap agreements on different tech indices rather than the stocks it holds. It's not the same thing even though it might involve a play on some of the same stocks. That can't be helped because the large-cap tech universe is limited, but holding the stocks separately allows the fund to really exploit price action to the upside.

If I had to say that another way, the swaps provide the leverage, but that leverage is not applied directly to TECL's portfolio of stocks. You have to appreciate this nuance because it provides quite a generous cushion or buffer against losses from the derivatives strategy, if any. Unfortunately, though, it also exposes the investor to a double drawdown when tech stocks move downward together because the equity exposure is compounded by the derivatives loss.

This isn't just theoretical. If you look at the semi-annual report (p. 129) for the six months ended April 30, 2025, you should be able to see a net investment income of about $11.7 million. That's seriously overshadowed by the net realized and unrealized losses totaling $863 million. The fund lost around $851 million for that period. That's marked by the April lows of the fund's NAV per share.

I'm not saying that the fund didn't work the way it should; on the contrary, the fund worked exactly how it should, but the market movement wasn't in a favorable direction for that half-year period. The fund still did its job of delivering a 3x return, but since the return was negative, the result of leverage was also negative, and this was amped up to three times what the equity part of the fund lost.

While we could see a significant improvement in the annual report that comes out next month for the year ending October 30, 2025 (yet unknown at the time of writing), this example illustrates how the volatility in the tech sector is amplified in this ETF.

Who Can Take Advantage of TECL?

Based on my assessment above, I see three investor types who might be interested in this kind of risky but potentially very rewarding leveraged ETF strategy:

  1. Tech investors who already hold much of the (QQQ) and want a booster shot when the going is good, meaning tech is on a strong rally and you want to grab what you can while tech is hot.
  2. Break-even seekers who have lost capital on tech bets and want to claw some of that back with an even more aggressive derivatives bet.
  3. Long-term investors looking for additional portfolio upside but also patient enough to weather tough periods like we saw in H1 2025.

Who Might Want to Avoid TECL?

Risk-averse investors, anyone with a loss-harvesting goal for the current tax year, and long-term investors who think tech valuations are frothy/bubbly and could re-rate downward rapidly in the foreseeable future.

In Closing

I'm not making any recommendation regarding TECL because this is an educational article for investors who would like to be more familiar with this leveraged ETP (exchange-traded product). Leverage is a double-edged sword. When used appropriately in a rallying tech market, it can significantly boost your portfolio's overall returns. When used indiscriminately without any regard for market momentum and macro factors, it can not only wipe out your investment in this product but also eat away large chunks of your current unrealized gains.

The decision comes down to two simple questions:

  1. Does this help me meet my investment goals?
  2. Can my portfolio tolerate this level of risk?

That's what it boils down to in the end – a good fitment with your overall strategy and an acceptance of the elevated risks that TECL brings to the table.

This article answers these three main questions about TECL:

  1. How does TECL work?
  2. What can investors expect from TECL in bull and bear periods for the tech sector?
  3. What kind of investor should invest in TECL?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Arko: Trapped Between Debt And Dealerization
data.description:
Arko (NASDAQ:ARKO) is one of those stocks that was cheap—and for good reason.

To put it in perspective, this US chain of convenience stores and gas stations is trading at around 12x EBITDA and an unbelievable 0.4x sales! Basically, that’s at least a 20% discount compared to peers like Murphy (MUSA) and as much as 70% compared to Casey's (CASY).

After all, the multiples are sending a pretty clear message to the market: Arko’s turned into a company that’s both stagnant and buried in debt.

And that's not exactly the position you want to be in.

But after a nearly 60% drop since it went public through a SPAC merger, you have to wonder if this selloff has gone a little too far.

But first, let's talk about the company:

  • Arko's story begins even before Arko itself. It all kicked off around 2003, when GPM Investments was founded and started running convenience stores in Virginia. At first, they really pushed into the Southeast, which is still their strongest area today, opening ~300 stores and spreading out into North Carolina and South Carolina.
  • When Arko (an Israeli holding company) became GPM's parent company in 2011–2012, the company had already more than doubled its footprint to approximately 700 stores. Arie Kotler—Arko's Chairman and CEO since 2005—took on a key role at GPM, a position he still holds today. Many analysts see the 2021–2023 period as the beginning of GPM's accelerated M&A strategy, but they forget that Arko had already been doing this at GPM for a long time before that. Between 2013–2019, the company made at least five acquisitions (Vickers Oil, Mountain Empire, E-Z Mart, Town & Country Food Stores, and Village Pantries), bringing the number of locations to approximately 1,300 and expanding into many other states.
  • In 2020, Arko set up in Delaware to smooth a merger with Haymaker Acquisition Corp. II, a SPAC. By the end of that year, the deal was done, and Arko—which used to trade on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange—was now listed on Nasdaq. A new round of M&A came in the 2021–2023 period (Empire Petroleum, Transit Energy Group, Quarles Petroleum, WTG Fuels, and Uncle's Convenience Stores), taking advantage of the approximately $300 million that was parked in cash.
  • Even after all those acquisitions, revenue stayed flat and margins got squeezed starting in FY 2022—not surprising during such a brutal inflationary period for both retail and gas stations. Arko then shifted gears to get back to profitability: zeroing in on its most profitable areas like fuel and fleet supply, refranchising retail locations, and putting more emphasis on organic growth and cutting down debt.

These days, even though growth has hit a wall (revenue peaked in FY 2023 and gross margins are still nowhere near pre-2022 levels), Arko’s still the sixth-largest convenience store operator in the US. The company runs about 3,700 locations across more than thirty states, including retail stores, fleet fueling sites, and gas stations.

The bulk of operations are in the Southeast (about 40% of the footprint), including over 300 stores in Virginia alone. The remainder is split between the Midwest (~25%), Northeast/Mid-Atlantic (~20%, primarily after FY 2021 and the $400 million acquisition of Empire Petroleum's retail and wholesale assets), and Southwest (~15%).

Arie Kotler remains the largest individual shareholder, with 18.5% of Arko's shares.

Debt: Arko's Achilles Heel

You can tell Arko’s debt is weighing on its valuation when there’s such a big gap between the multiples that factor in enterprise value and the ones that don’t.

Just take a look:

And the reason is pretty simple—M&A.

In the period I like to call Arko's 'early expansion' (2013–2019), they poured ~$252.4 million into acquisitions. At this point, $195.8 million in debt has been issued, bringing them to $218.7 million in total debt on the balance sheet.

Post-SPAC (2020–2023 period), approximately $1.48 billion was used for acquisitions. By the end of FY 2023, debt reached $828.6 million (more than quadrupled since FY 2019), with Debt/EBITDA at 3.1x.

Both revenue and EBITDA peaked in this 2022–2023 period but have been losing steam as discretionary spending tightened.

As debt rose, Arko's value creation could not keep up with the cost of debt and ended up destroying value on the bottom line.

Looking more precisely at the debt, we can see that:

  • More than half of the debt is Senior Notes with a fixed rate of 5.125%, the cheapest of all. Approximately 40% of the debt (the second largest portion) is a credit line with Capital One at a variable rate of SOFR + 2.25–3.25% secured by all of GPMP's assets.
  • 94% of the debt matures in 2028–2029, indicating a possible refinancing risk if we see re-inflation.
  • All the covenants are comfortably covered, including the ones tied to the Senior Notes.

Two Possible Paths

Bulls usually hang their hopes on two main ideas when it comes to Arko:

  1. Sale of the Retail segment, freeing up approximately $2 billion that could be used to clean up the balance sheet, buy back shares, or even pay special dividends. In this case, Arko would become a pure-play fuel company and would likely see margin gains and even a re-rating.
  2. A turnaround driven by dealerization. In this case, the company keeps turning its retail locations into something closer to franchises, stepping back from day-to-day operating costs and focusing more on supply and other higher-margin parts of the business.

Let’s start with the sale—the $2 billion figure isn’t something I’m making up. That number actually came from the company itself after a few strategy sessions with Citigroup’s bankers. Maybe they're just holding out for the right buyer or even in talks with debtholders about an early buyback.

Right off the bat, you can see that the $2 billion figure is equivalent to 1.1x FY 2024 sales. This is high when compared to the ~0.4x multiple that BizBuySell targets for c-stores or even that paid for 7-Eleven's (OTCPK:SVNDY) acquisition of 3,800 Speedway stores.

Since the Asking Price is usually ~33% higher on average than the Selling Price, I will consider a multiple of ~0.7x as a second option ($1.23 billion).

As I said before, Arko could do one or more of the following:

  1. Repay debt. With the bulk of the debt (~$790 million) maturing in the next three to four years, Arko could repay it without any problems in both cases. They would still have between $430 million and $1.2 billion to do whatever they want—from more acquisitions to returning capital now by running a debt-free company. Remember, this should increase profits by at least $50–90 million in interest savings annually, making it much more profitable.
  2. Pay a hefty special dividend. It doesn't happen often, but recently, another company I cover called Brightstar Lottery (BRSL) sold its iGaming business and used the proceeds to drastically reduce its debt while still returning $3 per share in a special dividend. Let's assume they use ~$400 million for this after eliminating long-term debt. With approximately $113.3 million shares outstanding, they could pay a special dividend of ~$3.50 per share (a whopping 87% yield!).
  3. Buy back shares more aggressively. Right now, their repurchase plan is pretty limited. But if they decided to really lean in (a ~$400 million program, for example), they could withdraw around 66 million shares at an average price of $6, which would translate to a roughly 60% shareholder yield.

All of these are solid options.

A sale could easily unlock more than $10 per share in my opinion, but I'm still not entirely convinced that's the route they'll take.

After all, they have already started making changes in retail—not selling, but transferring ownership to operators.

Being more realistic here and considering a deal between 0.4–0.7x sales, we would be talking about a much lower sale price than the one announced last year (between ~$700 million and $1.23 billion), which could limit the benefits to interest savings (if they choose to pay down all debt), which would yield a benefit of ~$0.80 per share annually and/or special dividends and smaller buybacks.

The market isn't pricing it in, probably because most investors don't really believe it will happen a year later.

At this point, I imagine Arko could undergo another re-rating and trade at an EV/S multiple of ~0.45x, in line with the low end, such as MLPs and integrated distributors like Par Pacific (PARR), Delek (DK), CrossAmerica (CAPL), and Sunoco (SUN).

Assuming revenues around $6.5 billion after retail sales, factoring in a continued but slower drop in fuel revenue, and zero debt, Arko could be valued somewhere close to $1.3 billion.

A strong upside to approximately $9.75. If you look closely, this is the upper end of Wall St. estimates.

The second potential route is dealerizing retail stores, which could push margins back up to their historical averages—or even higher.

But first, here's some color on that:

  1. So far, Arko has converted ~300 stores to this ownership model.
  2. By the end of FY 2025, Arko plans to convert an additional 200 locations, bringing the total to ~500 by the end of the year (about 13.5% of the total footprint).
  3. Converting roughly 300 stores has generated a cumulative annualized operating profit boost of more than $20 million.
  4. Management recently said it identified structural savings of $10 million in SG&A (1% reduction if we consider TTM numbers).

I'm not talking about them selling the retail division or handing it over entirely to operators. The way I see it, they could just improve efficiency and get back to 5-year average margins with some structural savings.

Let's assume they achieve revenue of ~$7.7 billion in FY 2026. Based on a 3% EBITDA margin, Arko could deliver EBITDA of $231 million. Applying a 12x EBITDA multiple and maintaining the current capital structure, we would find a price target of $4.

Now, let's imagine they achieve an EBITDA margin of ~4% in FY 2026 driven by optimization initiatives. Since Wall St. sees revenue falling in FY 2026 ($7.5 billion), EBITDA would be $300 million.

With the market already losing patience, we could expect a 10x EBITDA multiple. This would give us a price target of $5.20 already discounted to present value.

Bottom Line

To be blunt, my rating on Arko is 'Hold'.

I don't believe in the sell thesis at this point for a few reasons:

  • It’s been a year since this rumor hit the media, and the stock price has already adjusted as if nothing were going to happen.
  • Insiders are still selling the stock. All signs point to the fact that we would probably see insider buying if a sale looked likely, especially since the stock would almost certainly jump after an announcement at a good price.

  • They are already moving down the dealerization path, speeding it up in 2025. This suggests they are likely to keep going in that direction rather than pursuing a sale.
  • Arko is the 6th-largest c-store operator in the US, with 3,700 locations. Selling the retail business would transform them into a mid-sized fuel company.
  • Selling now, with compressed margins and a difficult macroeconomic environment, means selling at the worst possible time. Perhaps they're aiming too high at $2 billion and are refusing to sell at more compressed market multiples.

If Arko is unsuccessful in increasing margins with dealerization, I see significant downside. However, in the base case, I imagine EBITDA margins of 3-4%, implying the stock trading at $4-5.20 (upside of at best 30%). However, I still believe they may consider a deal closer to the refinancing in 2028-2029.

This would provide a massive upside if it comes out to between $1-2 billion.

Meanwhile, I see dealerization as a driver for margin improvements, so I expect the stock to remain relatively flat for now, with the market already priced in and resistance forming around $4. Add that to the ~2% yield and some low single digits in buybacks, and you have low to mid-single-digit annual returns.

So you can probably guess that I would see clear 'Buy' triggers as either a concrete announcement of a sale at an attractive price or dealerization pushing EBITDA margins above at least 3.5%.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Altria: Packs A Stable Punch Amid Macro Unease, 6.5% Dividend Yield
data.description:
The S&P 500’s bull market is set to turn three years old this weekend. Over that stretch, U.S. large caps have nearly doubled on a total return basis. Consumer Staples, however, has been largely left out of the upside move, but not all Staples stocks.

Today, I’m revisiting a major blue-chip winner since early 2024: Altria (NYSE:MO). I had a "Buy" rating on the now $110 billion market cap tobacco industry stock. Like many of its peers, shares are up substantially since my June 2024 analysis—the return, dividends included, is 65%.

I reiterate a "Buy" rating on the stock, which now sports a 6.45% forward dividend yield. The P/E ratio remains modest, while earnings growth should be steady in the years to come. I’ll also note where I see MO heading in the months ahead based on technical trends.

MO Marches Higher As XLP Sputters Since The Bull Market Began In October 2022

Back in July, Altria reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $1.44 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $1.38, while revenue of $6.1 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a strong $910 million beat.

Soft sales numbers were largely the result of lower revenue from its smokable products segment, but there were gains in its oral tobacco products segment (NJOY and on! Drove significant gains with a robust 26.5% shipment volume increase YoY). The management team then raised the lower end of its 2025 full-year EPS guidance range.

Oral Tobacco Growth

Shares rallied 3.6% in the session that followed, its best post-earnings performance since October 2024. Ahead of Q3 results due out on Oct. 30, the options market prices in a small 3.4% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date.

Looking back at the quarter that was, the company kept puffing out solid profit growth despite challenges in the tobacco industry. Altria managed to raise prices without cutting into broader growth, particularly in its nicotine pouch business. That helped to offset the weakness in cigarettes and smokeless-tobacco volume.

MO has long rewarded shareholders, but the management team noted that buyback activity could slow in the back half of 2025 compared to the same period last year. In 1H, over $4 billion was returned to stockholders via dividends and buybacks.

Still, operating income growth of 5.5% was encouraging, and current free cash flow levels appear sound for ongoing share repurchases and the lofty dividend. For the upcoming quarterly report, buyback plans will be interesting, while the margin story will be in focus.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be slow and steady in 2025 and throughout the years. The current 6%-plus rate may ease, while revenues hold steady just above the $20 billion mark. The sell-side, meanwhile, has turned markedly more bullish on Altria’s earnings potential—there have been a high 11 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days compared to just a lone downgrade.

Finally, profitability trends are healthy for the tobacco stock. FCF per share now clocks in at $5.16, which is a 7.7% FCF yield.

Altria: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates since my Q2 assessment. If we assume normalized operating EPS of $5.60 (near the current consensus for FY 2026) and apply the stock’s long-term average 12-13x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $70.

That doesn’t leave much upside, but investors are paid a large dividend to own the stock. A 12.5x P/E is also more than 10 figures cheaper than the S&P 500’s multiple. A new FDA regulatory plan should support a slight valuation premium compared to MO's long-term average.

MO: A Higher P/E Today, But A Still-Strong Yield

Key risks for Altria include, of course, ongoing challenges in the U.S. nicotine industry, along with the potential for crackdowns and tighter regulations from the Trump Administration and Secretary Kennedy.

The company also called out that inflation has been a key threat on the Q2 earnings call. Thus, persistent 3%-plus annual consumer price increases would be problematic. A weakening consumer environment could weigh on margins—listen for clues on that topic in the Q3 report.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2025 earnings date of Thursday, Oct. 30 BMO.

Be on the lookout for an update to the current stock buyback program, as it's set to end on Dec. 31. Altria raised its dividend back in August to $1.06 per share quarterly.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares approaching full value, but with a high free cash flow and dividend yield, the technical situation appears strong. Notice in the chart below that shares are now within earshot of the all-time high of the 2017 high in the upper $70s. The trend since Q1 2024 is obviously higher, and the long-term 200-day moving average is firmly on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the blue measured move upside price target. Based on the height of the 2022 through 2024 drop and recovery, an objective of $76 is in play. On the downside, there is an uptrend support line that is right near the 50dma, so that should offer some support.

Furthermore, a symmetrical triangle apex (orange) has confluence with that zone close to the $60 mark. That would be an opportunity to buy the stock at a better discount to fair value than today’s level. Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under the current level, there should be ample support if we see a dip.

MO: Motoring Higher, 2017 Peak In Play, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on MO, and I am lifting my price target to $70. The discount to fair value is now quite narrow, but the high dividend yield and strong technicals are positive considerations.

data.title: TSMC Heads Into Q3 With Strong Momentum
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage in June Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) stock has jumped by almost 40%, well outpacing the market as the investors have recognized its leadership in the 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm nodes. However, the valuation has not fully reflected its true potential, as the forward P/S ratio is 56% off the 5-year mean.

Leading up to Q3 earnings on October 16, investors are forecasting another robust print as AI demand continues to support 3nm ramp and client dependence grows. Although geopolitical and forex risk remain, TSMC’s yield leadership cements its position as the indispensable foundry of the AI era.

TSMC’s Market Lead in Advanced Nodes Pushes Topline Growth

TSMC has a lead in advanced semiconductor node-tech, mainly in 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm processes. This node lead provides stable stock price growth through capitalizing on the high demand for AI/HPC chips. The trends in revenue acceleration and increasing contribution from advanced nodes (amid AI-demand tailwinds) led to margin and market share expansion. The revenue trends (monthly) for FY25 point out that TSMC's YTD revenue through September hit NT$2,762,964 million with a 36.4% YoY increase (from NT$2,025,846 million in 9M-FY24).

There is a noticeable structural change in this growth. Revenue increased 36.3% year over year to NT$839,254 million in Q1-FY25 from NT$615,644 million in Q1-FY24. Q2-FY25 increased by 38.6% YoY to NT$933,792 million. With monthly breakdowns of July NT$323,166 million, August NT$335,772 million, and September NT$330,980 million, Q3-FY25 totalled NT$989,918 million (US$34,135 million). This performance, which represents a 30.3% YoY increase, exceeded guidance.

Quarterly growth has remained persistently in double digits throughout, averaging well above 35% YoY during 2025. That's significantly above the pre-2023 average of around 20–25%, marking the much steeper expansion phase. Revenue went up to NT$2,263,891 million during 2022 from NT$1,587,415 million during 2021 by 42.6%, but that was fueled by post-pandemic recovery rather than by AI demands.

The topline acceleration in FY24-FY25 was in line with the AI boom. Historically, the topline growth was stabilizing at 15-20% CAGR from FY19-FY23 (2019: NT$1,069,985 million to 2023: NT$2,161,736 million, ~20% CAGR), but now it has jumped to 33.8% in 2024 (full-year NT$2,894,308 million) and projected ~30% for FY25 based on guidance.

In short, TSMC's Q3-FY25 sales beat estimates amid strong AI chip demand, with September revenue up 31.4% YoY. The street is estimating double-digit topline growth to continue in the upcoming years under strong AI demand. The market is forecasting WFE (wafer fab equipment) growth to $122 billion in FY26 as TSMC is capturing market share due to AI accelerators.

However, the consensus-based topline growth is not in line with the same level of growth seen in FY25. It is making TSM stock undervalued in terms of topline valuation.

Fundamentally, TSMC's moat is coming from advanced node capabilities. For instance, in Q2-FY25, 3nm processes contributed 24% of wafer revenue (up from 15% YoY and 22% QoQ), 5nm 36% (stable but high-margin), and 7nm 14% (down from 17% YoY as migration to finer nodes occurs). These Advanced tech nodes (7nm and below) accounted for 74% of total wafer revenue that is up from ~65% in prior quarters. This tech shift is vital as finer nodes enable denser and more efficient chips ideal for AI training/inference. So, TSMC is commanding premium pricing with that.

The premium pricing is boosting margins directly as Q2 actual gross margin hit 58.6% (beating guidance midpoint of 58%), and the operating margin hit 49.6% (beating 47-49%). AI chips from clients like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) (H20, Blackwell) and Huawei (Ascend 910C using TSMC dies) are driving this growth. As per Counterpoint Research, TSMC's foundry market share jumped to 38% in Q2 2025 (from 31% YoY) based on AI demand on 3nm/ packaging like CoWoS.

Overall, these revenue trends point to advanced nodes ramping quickly. For instance, 5nm in Q2-FY22 led to ~21% topline contribution and hit 36% by Q2-FY25. Whereas 3nm led 6% topline in Q3-FY23 and is on a stronger growth path (24% in Q2-FY25). Also, TSMC has a client lock-in ecosystem of chips. How? Nvidia and Intel Corporation (INTC) rely on TSMC for core AI chips, and there are deals like the one with Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) for Tomahawk Ultra.

This client lock-in solidifies TSMC’s moat as clients like Apple (80% portfolio on 3nm) and Qualcomm (2nm launches) depend on TSMC's chip yield superiority (Samsung struggles with 3nm yields). Thus, a higher advanced node mix in the topline may lead to revenue per wafer growth based on a premium for 3nm vs. 7nm. The increasing mix of advanced nodes can continue to boost TSMC’s overall topline despite flat unit volumes.

Geopolitical Drama and Supply Chain Issues Boost Operational Risks

A main downside for TSMC is its continued exposure to AI-chip geopolitics and supply chain issues. Here, the US-China trade war, Trump tariffs, and rare earth dependencies are leading to adverse policy issues like China's rare earth export rules. These export rules are pushing costs higher and possibly compressing margins for TSMC. Further, geopolitical risks are vital in the case of TSMC’s fab as China is tightening rare earth export rules (with a control of 90% on global supply). So, these rules hit hard on wafer fabrication (TSMC's core) cost-wise through price hikes on inputs.

In the past, since 2019, Huawei's bans, TSMC's China revenue has dropped from 16% in Q2-FY24 to 9% in Q2-FY25 that points to forced diversification and lost volume. Similarly, now, the US has revoked TSMC's VEU authorization for the Nanjing facility (effective from December), and it requires licenses for shipments. These types of regulatory shifts can considerably hurt TSMC’s chip capacity on mature nodes and can lead to rerouting of shipments with higher costs.

Additionally, supply chain issues also boost the bottom-line weakness. How? TSMC's fab ecosystem relies on global inputs for raw materials. Huawei's use of TSMC dies and SK Hynix HBM despite bans points to possible circumvention risks and following regulatory scrutiny. Pentagon's MP Materials partnership to offset China's rare earth monopoly makes TSMC's cost exposure larger. For instance, 2020-2021 supply shortages (COVID/China tensions) caused 10%-20% wafer price hikes. But now reversed, here, FY25 capex $38-$42 billion may hit hard if disruptions hit the bottom line.

Finally, Q3 outlook points to forex swings. Q2 actual 31.05 and 29 in Q3 boosted USD topline but compressed margins (every 1% NTD appreciation cuts TSMC’s revenue by 1%). TSMC is raising $10 billion for its offshore unit to hedge, but it can be ineffective against policy shocks. So, geopolitics can drive topline volatility through forex also as China's rules weaken USD/strengthen NTD. As a result, it can erode the export edge.

Forward Earnings Still Attractive Despite the Rally

Even off the back of a 40% rally since June, the valuation for TSMC looks fair when tied to forward earnings. Consensus calls for FY25 EPS of $9.85, putting the forward P/E up at 30.4x, and FY26 EPS of $11.5, bringing the multiple down to 26.1x, suggesting strong YoY growth of ~17% into FY26, well above sector median. Importantly, despite the recent rally, these multiples remain supported by accelerating revenue, expected to reach $120.7 billion in FY25 and $142.1 billion in FY26, reflecting sustained AI and HPC demand.

Compared to its 5-year average forward P/E of ~22.6x, TSM's premium is justified by structural node leadership and client lock-ins. With the stock still carrying a forward P/S ratio 56% below its 5-year average, valuation appears undemanding relative to growth potential. In short, earnings momentum into FY26 provides a cushion for multiple expansion.

Takeaway

Due to demand driven by AI, TSMC has surged since June, but its valuation remains attractive. Its advanced-node leadership and client lock-ins justify a premium, as revenue is expected to accelerate over $140 billion and EPS is expected to grow approximately 17% into FY26. Earnings momentum supports additional upside despite geopolitical risks.

data.title: Mousetraps: 6 High-Yield REITs Risking Dividend Cuts
data.description:
The temptation always exists to reach for high yield as a source of income and a buffer against volatility. But the yield you see is not always the yield you get. Companies can and will cut their dividends at any time, though they generally avoid doing so as long as possible.

Even in the best of times, there are always mousetrap REITs. These companies offer alluringly high dividend yields, but in reality, those yields often turn out to be an illusion. In fact, sometimes the reason the yield is so high is precisely because the company is doing poorly, and shares have sold off, but the dividend cut hasn't happened yet.

I published a similar list last August and another last April. However, the list has changed, and it's always a good idea to check again periodically to make sure your portfolio is free of mousetraps.

The group of Mousetraps I identified in December of 2023 lost (-6.54)% on average in 2024, while the average REIT gained 14.40%. So those Mousetraps underperformed by a whopping 2094 bps (basis points).

Here's how the August 2024 mousetraps fared over the ensuing 12 months:

Ticker Open Close Gain YAP Div. Pd YOC Total Return
GNL $8.30 $ 6.88 (-17.1) % 13.25% $ 0.93 11.20% (-5.9)%
CLPR $4.05 $ 3.44 (-15.1)% 9.38% $0.38 9.38% (-5.7)%
GMRE $ 46.45 $ 33.75 (-27.3)% 9.04% $3.90 8.40% (-18.9)%
DEA $ 33.13 $ 21.82 (-34.1)% 8.00% $ 2.23 6.71% (-27.4)%
HR $ 17.57 $ 16.54 (-5.9)% 7.06% $1.24 7.06% 1.16%
PSTL $ 14.59 $13.70 (-6.1)% 6.58% $0.97 6.61% 0.50%
CCI $113.45 $106.88 (-5.8)% 5.52% $5.76 5.08% (-0.72)%
Average (-15.9)% 8.40% 7.73% (-8.17)%
VNQ $ 91.10 $ 88.92 (-2.4)% 3.57% $3.47 3.81% 1.41%

Source: MarketWatch.com, Seeking Alpha Premium

Total Return = Gain + Yield.

It was an uninspiring 12 months for the VNQ, which managed a total return of just 1.41%, thanks to a (-2.4)% gain in share price and a YOC (yield on cost) of 3.81%. But the Mousetraps fared considerably worse, posting an average total return of (-8.17)%, as share price slid (-15.9)%, almost twice as much as their high 7.73% average YOC paid after their respective cuts. Notably, their YOC (actual yield, or yield on cost) was 67 bps below their YAP (yield at purchase).

Bottom line: The Mousetrap REITs underperformed again, this time by a substantial 958 bps, and likely almost always will. The risk is real and significant.

Dividend Safety: Why it's important

Dividend safety is crucial to the value of a REIT for one simple reason. When a REIT cuts its dividend, COWhand investors dump shares like a load of ripe garbage, and the share price plummets. The mousetrap has sprung, leaving the hapless investor with the worst of both worlds: Reduced income and a dramatic loss in share value, often leaving little recourse but to sell at a loss or else hold for a very long time in hopes of a comeback. Thus, it's vitally important to avoid mousetrap REITs, and if you are holding one, it's prudent to sell it before management cuts the dividend.

Dividend Safety: How to Read the scores

Fortunately, Seeking Alpha Premium supplies investors with a Dividend Safety score, balancing up to 20 data points for each company. According to Seeking Alpha, over a 15-year test period (2010-2024), 70% of companies that earned an F for Dividend Safety cut their dividends, and 41% cut dividends within 12 months after receiving the F rating. Nearly all (93%) of dividend cuts came from companies rated C+ or lower, whereas companies with a Dividend Safety rating of B- or better ran only a 2% risk of a cut, and that risk fell to 1% for companies rated A- or better.

Based on the 40% figure supplied by Seeking Alpha, we would expect 2.8 of last year's seven Mousetraps to have cut their dividends. However, instead of the expected two or three, there were four companies on the list that cut dividends during the 12-month period following publication of the article.

  • GNL cut its dividend by 31%, beginning with the April 2025 payout.
  • GMRE cut its dividend by 28.6%, beginning with the June 2025 payout.
  • DEA cut its dividend by 32.1%, beginning with the May 2025 payout.
  • CCI cut its dividend by 32.1%, beginning with the June 2025 payout.

So, 57% of companies with a Dividend Safety grade of F did cut their dividends over that 12-month period. In addition, although HR did not cut its dividend during the 12-month period in question, it did cut its dividend by 22.6% shortly after, beginning with the August 2025 payout.

By contrast, CLPR kept the dividend unchanged, while PSTL increased its dividend slightly (1%) beginning with February 2025.

Dividend Yield: How high is high enough?

With two-year and 10-year U.S. Treasuries offering investors anywhere from 3.58% to 4.10% at virtually no risk, the question for an income investor is this: How much additional yield do you need to justify owning a risk asset such as a REIT? With the average REIT yielding 3.86%, highly competitive with the no-risk rate, about half of the REITs are not worth owning, unless the underlying stock price is set to rise faster than the market. If you know how to spot and time those, more power to you. Most of us don't.

Every investor will set his or her own yield cut-off. For the purposes of this article, I will define high yield as 5.25% or better. That's at least 115 bps above the no-risk rate.

Mouse Trap List: REITs in the Danger Zone

Yields change on a daily basis, and Dividend Safety grades also change over time, but as I write this, there are 22 equity REITs with yields over 5.25% and dividend safety ratings in the Danger Zone. They're listed below, in descending order by yield.

Company Ticker Yield Div. Safety
Community Healthcare Trust CHCT 12.58% D-
Gladstone Commercial GOOD 9.87% F
Clipper Realty CLPR 9.48% D-
Global Net Lease GNL 9.28% F
One Liberty Properties OLP 8.10% D-
Armada Hoffler AHH 8.02% D
Easterly Government Properties DEA 7.99% D
Modiv Industrial MDV 7.88% D
Brandywine Realty BDN 7.80% F
National Storage Affiliates NSA 7.55% D
Saul Centers BFS 7.48% D-
Alexander's ALX 7.39% F
Braemar Hotels & Resorts BHR 7.38% D-
Omega Healthcare Investors OHI 6.51% D
NexPoint Residential NXRT 6.45% D-
BRT Apartments BRT 6.38% D-
UMH Properties UMH 6.18% D
Highwoods Properties HIW 6.17% D
Gladstone Land LAND 6.07% D-
CBL & Associates Properties CBL 6.01 D-
Medical Properties Trust MPW 5.78% F
Healthcare Realty HR 5.37% F

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium

The remainder of this article will concentrate on the six REITs with Dividend Safety grades of F to take a quick look at why the Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings system considers the risk of a dividend cut so high. Remember, D is for Danger.

Seeking Alpha Premium provides 14 dividend safety-related data points for Brandywine Realty (BDN). Of these, 11 grade out at D or worse. Although the cash dividend payout ratio looks fine (grade C), payout as a percentage of AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) is well over 100%, earning an F, as do the company's astronomical long debt/EBITDA and long debt/assets ratios. Although Brandywine just cut its dividend by nearly 50%, beginning with the next payout, there's still palpable danger of potentially another cut sometime in the next 12 months.

FFO per share for BDN nosedived by (-47)% in 2024, with another (-32)% drop expected in 2025, while share price plummeted (-30.0)% over the TTM (trailing 12 months). Shares now trade at just 6.9x FFO '25.

The Quant system provides 14 data points for Global Net Lease (GNL). Of these, 11 grade out at D+ or worse. The company's exorbitant FFO payout ratios are especially problematic. Long debt/assets is also substantially worse than the sector average. GNL has cut its dividend three times in the past two years and still appears to be at risk of cutting again.

With a 68% debt ratio and 35.7% of its debt held at variable rates, the tempting near-double-digit yield seemingly depends on an immediate and substantial surge in revenues.

Unfortunately, GNL's nosedived (-58.3)% in FFO in 2024 and is on pace to dive another (-50.0)% in 2025. Meanwhile, the share price declined only (-3.41)% TTM, so the bait is working. Shares are selling at 16.0x FFO'25.

Of the 13 data points provided for Alexander's (ALX), five grade out at D or worse. Total debt to equity and to capital are particularly concerning, as are the payout ratios. FFO to total debt is near zero. Meanwhile, the company's long debt to assets and EBITDA earn high marks.

FFO per share for ALX declined (-17.6)% in 2024 and is on pace to decline another (-14.4)% this year, while the company's price per share actually rose by 6.06% TTM. Shares now sell at a slightly frothy 20.1x FFO '25.

Of the 17 data points provided for Gladstone Commercial (GOOD), eight grade at D or worse. All the company's payout ratios are challenging (except AFFO TTM), and long debt/assets is particularly troublesome.

FFO per share for GOOD slumped by (-7.8)% in 2024 but is expected to come in essentially flat (+1.04%) for 2025. Meanwhile, share price has plummeted (-27.06)% TTM, now selling at 8.1x FFO '25.

Of the 15 data points the Quant system supplies for Medical Properties Trust (MPW), 10 grade at D or worse. Although the FFO payout ratios look good, the forward AFFO payout ratio is weak. Meanwhile, long debt/EBITDA, FFO/total debt, and FFO/revenue are abysmal. MPW has already sliced its dividend nearly in half on two occasions since June 2023. That doesn't mean they won't potentially cut again.

FFO per share for MPW nosedived (-53.0)% in 2024 and is on pace to swoon another (-30.0)% this year. Share price has held surprisingly steady at (-3.5)% TTM, now representing 9.6x FFO '25.

For Healthcare Realty (HR), the Quant system provides 15 data points. While only six of them grade out at D or worse, four of them are F's. These include the AFFO and cash dividend payout ratios TTM and interest coverage at just 0.20.

FFO per share for HR slumped by (-7.7)% in 2024 but is expected to remain nearly flat at +1.9% in 2025. Share price has risen by a modest 3.25% TTM, now selling at 11.3x FFO '25.

The Bottom Line

Dividend Safety is only one factor of many to consider when investing. A grade of F on Dividend Safety means there's about a 40% chance of a dividend cut within the next 12 months. However, a Hold case can be made for some of the companies mentioned in this article, even the seven with the worst Dividend Safety ratings. A Buy case can even be made on a few.

The Seeking Alpha Quant system is not necessarily the final word on dividend safety, but the fact that these companies grade out so low in that system should give a sensible investor cause to pause before adding more shares and good reason to seriously consider cutting exposure and reallocating capital.

And remember, the opinion that matters most is yours. Because it's your money.

data.title: Aixtron: Near-Term Outlook Is Negative
data.description:
Investment Summary

I recommend a hold rating on Aixtron (OTCPK:AIXXF) as the near-term headwinds in its core power electronics markets offset what I see as compelling long-term opportunities tied to AI-driven optics and GaN power adoption. While AIXXF leadership in MOCVD technology and strong positioning in optoelectronics are great, order softness and falling backlog indicate that the current downcycle still has room to play out.

Business Overview

AIXXF is a Germany-based semiconductor equipment maker specializing in Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition [MOCVD] and Chemical Vapor Deposition [CVD] systems, both essential for producing advanced compound semiconductors. AIXXF tools enable the manufacturing of next-generation devices across two key markets: power electronics (covering silicon carbide [SiC] and gallium nitride [GaN] applications for EVs, renewable energy, and data center power systems) and optoelectronics (including lasers for AI data communications, 3D sensing, and high-performance LEDs). AIXXF has a global customer base that includes integrated device manufacturers [IDM] and specialized wafer producers.

Pockets of Strength

AIXXF is experiencing very solid momentum in its optoelectronics segment, whose strength is being driven by the explosive surge in data traffic from AI, hyperscale data centers, and 5G infrastructure. I don’t foresee any slowdown in this data traffic for the coming decade as the world continues to digitalize. The result is that network bandwidth needs to double roughly every two years (source: Q2 earnings), and the current optical infrastructure simply cannot support this. As such, the industry is undergoing a shift toward advanced optical technologies such as co-packaged optics [CPO] and photonic integrated circuits [PICs]. Without going into the technicalities (which readers can do so by Googling), in short, these technologies enable faster, more energy-efficient data transmission. And adoption of these technologies directly benefits AIXXF, as its G10-AsP system has become the “tool of record” (words by management from the earnings call) for producing the laser components.

The adoption trends speak for themselves. Management has highlighted strong customer traction, including new engagements with top-tier names like Nokia and expanded orders from a major global customer’s US operations. This clearly signals growing adoption of AIXXF optical solutions. Importantly, AIXXF expects optoelectronics (especially laser-related applications) to account for roughly one-third of total 2025 order intake, and that provides both strong revenue visibility and a solid buffer against cyclical weakness in the other segments.

Looking further out, there is a major growth opportunity for AIXXF exposure to AI data centers' power needs. The way I see it, a data center's power system is like a city's electrical grid. The old way was less efficient. Now, Nvidia (NVDA) is leading a shift to a new, more powerful 800-volt standard called Kyber, set to roll out around 2027. This new architecture relies on advanced materials like GaN because they handle high power much more efficiently than traditional silicon, reducing energy waste. And here is where AIXXF benefits. AIXXF makes the essential epitaxy machines that build these GaN chips, and it dominates this market with a ~90% share. As data centers adopt this new standard to power AI, which I think they will since power is such a big factor in operating data centers, the industry will need a massive number of GaN devices, which would create a significant new wave of demand for AIXXF tools. While this growth is a few years away, it represents a powerful long-term catalyst.

A Period of Digestion and Uncertainty

While the long-term growth story is attractive, the near-term reality for AIXXF is tough. It is currently navigating one of the most pronounced cyclical downturns in its core power electronics markets in recent history. Both the SiC and GaN segments are in what management calls a “longer digestion period,” as customers work through the heavy overcapacity built up during the 2021–2023 investment cycle. Unfortunately, many chipmakers significantly overestimated near-term demand, and that led to a wave of new fab builds that are now sitting underutilized. The situation is most acute in SiC, where management noted that the installed base in China alone could theoretically meet global demand two to three times over.

So, in China, we have seen over the last two, three years a massive build out in silicon carbide wafer production capacity. I think China today has 6-inch wafer production capacities which is probably good enough to serve 2x or 3x the world market demand, yes? So, in 6-inch we have in China a massive, I say truly massive over capacity. There's literally entire factories filled with tools and they're all standing idle and being turned off...we are still in the point where our customers have a decent amount of idle capacity, unused capacities, both on the silicon carbide and in the gallium nitride. Q2 2025 earnings transcript

While Asia provided some cushion earlier in the year, it is just not enough to offset the ongoing pressure. The slowdown is evident in AIXXF order metrics. For 1H 2025, total order intake declined 15% y/y to EUR250.7 million, and in Q2 specifically, the drop got worse, falling to 33% y/y, reaching EUR118.5 million. This directly impacted the backlog, which now stands at EUR284.6 million at the end of Q2, down 29% y/y. As such, while the optoelectronics segment provided some near-term visibility and buffer, the reality is the AIXXF backlog has contracted significantly, and this clearly signals that near-term revenue will remain soft.

Also, while AIXXF remains a dominant player in epitaxy machines, it doesn’t mean competition is not intensifying. In SiC epitaxy, companies like ASM International (OTCQX:ASMIY) are making meaningful inroads, particularly with IDMs seeking diversified supply chains. However, the bigger threat, in my view, comes from China’s aggressive localization push. Beijing’s policy initiatives are explicitly aimed at reducing reliance on imported semiconductor tools, with significant subsidies flowing to domestic players. These Chinese MOCVD and epitaxy toolmakers are catching up technologically. As local tools continue to improve, the risk is that Chinese IDMs will increasingly favor domestic suppliers, putting downward pressure on AIXXF market share and margins.

Valuation

All in all, I think there are certainly areas to be positive about for the AIXXF equity story. The accelerating demand for AI data centers is unlikely to pause anytime soon, and AIXXF is in a very good position to win demand from this. However, the fact is also that orders are down (at the consolidated level), and backlog is down accordingly. The near-term outlook for AIXXF's core business is just not great. It is also unclear when the recovery will happen since a lot of factories are still sitting idle (which means a lot of spare capacity). As such, I think it justifies AIXXF to trade at a discount to history, as it is still in the “down cycle.” At 17.5x NTM PE, I think it's fair since AIXXF traded at an average of 24x over the past 5 years. Multiples should remain range-bound until there is clarity on the timing of recovery.

Conclusion

I recommend a hold rating for AIXXF. While the long-term catalysts, particularly AI data center power demand and the shift toward GaN-based architectures, are strong, the near-term setup remains challenged by weak order trends and rising competitive pressures. The stock is likely to trade sideways until signs of recovery emerge, especially as customers digest excess capacity built in prior years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
